Post_Text: You put an officer on campus to make kids safer. You may have just bought a lawsuit your policy won't touch. Here's the gap that opens the day a sworn officer starts working your halls: [LINK]
You put an officer on campus to keep kids safe. The two policies you trust most are both written to walk away from what happens next. (Full newsletter, see linkedin-newsletter.md)

A hallway incident becomes a federal civil rights complaint. It names the officer, the department, and your school. You call your broker, sure this is what insurance is for. Read what that call sounds like for most schools: [LINK]

Your two main liability policies are both written to walk away from a use-of-force claim. General liability excludes law enforcement. Educators legal liability excludes intentional acts. Guess where an SRO incident lands: [LINK]
Have a School Resource Officer on campus? The two policies you carry most are usually written to exclude exactly the claim an officer creates. (Checklist + matrix, see company-page-checklist.md)
Notes: Post from company page; consider PDF lead-capture version

Post_Text: "The officer works for the police department, so we're clear." A plaintiff's attorney doesn't name only the officer. They name the department, and they name your school. Here's why being pulled into the lawsuit is close to automatic: [LINK]

There's a line of coverage built specifically for what an officer does on your campus. It exists because general liability shuts law enforcement out. Most schools with an SRO have never been told it exists: [LINK]

The single most important risk document in your SRO arrangement is the MOU. It decides who indemnifies whom and who carries the insurance. An administrator and a police chief sign it. Your broker is never in the room. That's the problem: [LINK]

One question for whoever handles your school's insurance. If an officer incident became a civil rights claim next month, do you know which policy responds, and have you confirmed it isn't one of the two written to exclude it? If that's a shrug, read this: [LINK]
Notes: Closes the campaign on the NEPQ question
